metadata.title: Claude, For Teachers
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 the one accent. Do not retint.
metadata.greeting_note: Liam guest-hosts this reel (am_onyx voice). Original Maori greeting retired for guest speaker clarity.
metadata.note: ILLUSTRATE LAW reel: UI only at B00 (with RESULT lines), B04 (Cowork IS the subject — window+callout legal), verdict, handoff, outro. B01 LayerStack / B02 standards-into-Cowork / B03 ConnectorGrid / B05 PredictCard. COLD OPEN, SPARK-LINE, HANDOFF, OUTRO laws in force. Source: Anthropic announcement 'Introducing Claude for Teachers', Jul 14 2026.
metadata.reformat: center-cut, focus-aware; pantry/<bid>-916.* is the human replacement slot; -916 overrides honored

beats.narration_text: Liam, in for Bear. Claude for Education just launched free access for teachers. Three layers: standards, tools, and agentic scheduling — all connected through Learning Commons.

props.command: I keep circling the Learning Commons scaffold — standards broken into ordered competencies feels like the real engine here. I want to trace one concrete lesson through it. Take a single seventh-grade ratios standard: (1) show me the competency chain underneath it in the order students usually learn it, (2) draft a lesson that cites Illustrative Mathematics for exactly one of those competencies, and (3) explain what the four-o'clock scheduled task would actually read from my exit-ticket folder to adapt tomorrow's plan.
output: a free tier — verified US K-12 educators
a skills library — open-source, on GitHub
connectors — state standards + nine classroom tools

beats.narration_text: Strip the branding and it's three layers. A free tier — premium Claude for verified educators. A library of teaching skills. And connectors. That's the whole product. Less like Claude Design — a product you go to — more like the Figma and Canva MCPs: plumbing into the Claude you already use.

The anchor connector is Learning Commons: academic standards for all fifty states — and underneath each one, the smaller competencies it's built from, in the order students usually learn them. Ask for a lesson plan, and Claude drafts from that scaffold, with OpenSciEd and Illustrative Mathematics attached as trusted sources.

Then the ecosystem: nine classroom tools connect on day one. Canva for Education, MagicSchool, Diffit, ASSISTments, Brisk, Coteach, Eedi, Snorkl, TeachFX. Same pattern as design tools getting MCP servers — the tools you already use become things Claude can drive.

And because the tier includes Cowork and Claude Code, it isn't just chat. Hand it a folder — roster, diagnostics, your own notes — and it maps where every student is. Hand off one task — review today's exit tickets, adapt tomorrow's plan — and it runs every school day at four. Claude works while you drive home. Their words — and the best line in the announcement.

One prediction before the verdict. The teaching skills — the actual pedagogy — are open-source on GitHub, and the fluency course is Creative Commons. Why would they give the best part away? Commit to an answer.

props.question: Why open-source the teaching skills?
props.commit: commit — then check yourself against the verdict

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The verdict — and your answer. They give the skills away because the content was never the moat. Being the front end is. Standards as data, tools as connectors, pedagogy as open source — and Claude in the middle. Free for verified U.S. K-12 educators, FERPA terms, and your data never trains the model.

artifactLines: A tier + skills + connectors on the Claude you have — the Figma/Canva MCP pattern, not a Claude Design-style app.
Learning Commons serves all-50-states standards as data; OpenSciEd + Illustrative Mathematics attached.
Nine classroom tools connect day one; the teaching skills are open-source — the moat is being the front end.
Educators only · FERPA DPA · no training on your data · free for verified US K-12 — sign up by June 30, 2027.

beats.narration_text: Your turn — and make it yours: your grade, your subject, your state. Paste this, and check the receipts it hands back — every part of the plan should name its standard. If you're verified, this costs you nothing.

props.command: I teach 7th-grade science in Ohio. Plan Monday's lesson from my state's standards, differentiated for three readiness levels — and show me which standard and curriculum each part came from.
